metadata.file_role: Porcini, king boletes, and brown allies
metadata.purpose: Porcini-type and related brown bolete records.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Bay Bolete is the canonical app record for Imleria badia. The former scientific-name slug imleria-badia is retained only as a search/former-slug alias.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.
records.edibility_detail: Use only after confirming the bay-brown cap, yellow pores that bruise grayish blue, conifer/decayed-wood setting, and non-reticulate wrinkled stem.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in northeastern North America and the upper Midwest.
mushroom_profile.summary: Bay-brown to reddish-brown cap, pale yellow pores that bruise grayish blue, wrinkled non-reticulate stem, and frequent fruiting near conifers or very decayed mossy wood.

records.field_identification: Bay-brown to reddish-brown cap, pale yellow pores that bruise grayish blue, wrinkled non-reticulate stem, and frequent fruiting near conifers or very decayed mossy wood.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Imleria badia
use_links.notes: Bay-brown cap, yellow pores that bruise blue, stem texture, and ecology.

records.look_alike_notes: Bitter bolete has pinkish pores and a very bitter taste; king bolete is usually more robust with a different reticulate stem pattern and does not show the same bay-bolete blue bruising pattern.
records.edibility_notes: Cooked food. Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.

edible_use.notes: Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.

images_structured.title: Bay bolete cap and pores
images_structured.author: Tintling
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (wave2-rights-known-table); source page retained for attribution.

images_structured.title: Bay bolete supporting field view
images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (credits-enrichment-v73); source page retained for attribution.

records.source_note: v4.3.211 removes contradictory duplicate metadata that hid the only canonical Bay Bolete profile; no separate imleria-badia record exists in the canonical loader.

records.notes: King Bolete / porcini is kept as a practical field-guide page, but North American Boletus edulis-like mushrooms are taxonomically messy. Treat this as an edulis-group/porcini-style entry unless a collection is confirmed to species level.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent sautéed, dried, or used in soups and sauces when young and firm. Avoid soft, old, bug-ridden specimens.
records.general_notes: Use as a group record for porcini/king bolete types in the region; do not treat every brown-capped bolete as king bolete.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall.

records.scientific_name: Boletus edulis group / North American porcini complex
records.duplicate_note: Duplicate King Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

images_structured.title: King bolete field view
images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (credits-enrichment-v70); source page retained for attribution.

records.foraging_value: Prime foraging — species group needs care
records.mushroom_card_note: Porcini lane: white flesh, no blue staining, no red pores, reticulate upper stem.
records.field_identification: Stout bolete with a brown bun-like cap, white pores when young becoming yellow-olive with age, thick pale stem often with fine white netting near the top, and firm white flesh that does not blue-stain.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.

records.quality_notes: Excellent when young and firm; quality drops fast when soft, old, buggy, or waterlogged.
records.eating_use_notes: Excellent sautéed, dried, or used in soups and sauces. Use only firm, clean specimens.
records.risk_reason: Some boletes cause severe GI reactions, and edible boletes can be confused with toxic, bitter, or poorly tolerated species.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness, and dehydration are possible with toxic/problem boletes.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Pores instead of gills can make beginners overconfident. Risk traits include red/orange pores, rapid blue staining, bitter taste, unusual staining, and lack of clear porcini traits.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat unknown boletes. Avoid red-pored boletes, bitter boletes, and rapidly blue-staining boletes unless identified to species by an expert.
records.caution_reason: Bolete ID still matters; “has pores” does not make it food.

lookalike_risk.label: High — poisonous/severe-GI and bitter bolete confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — poisonous/severe-GI and bitter bolete confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Porcini-type boletes are food only when bitter Tylopilus, false king bolete, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining boletes are ruled out.
required_checks: Confirm white young pores aging yellow/olive
Confirm pale netting near upper stem when expected
Reject pinkish pores plus bitter taste
Reject strong or inconsistent blue/green-blue staining
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat any bolete that does not match the full porcini package.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.

records.culinary_uses: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation. Keep it for recognition/comparison until source review is cleared.

mushroom_profile.summary: Source-limited porcini-type entry for Almost Bluing King Bolete.
staining: Weak or limited blue bruising possible
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September.
research_notes: Still needs a strong species-level non-Wikipedia source for morphology and edible-use review before source debt can be cleared.
Consumer-app/site leads tie the names Bluing King Bolete, Blueing King Bolete, and Blue-staining porcini to Boletus subcaerulescens; kept here as search aliases only, not source-of-record support.
Food-route demoted in v4.3.175 until stronger species-level edible-use support is attached.

records.general_notes: Source/status cleanup v4.3.170: record remains visible but review-needed; current attached data does not yet clear species-level edible use.
records.non_edible_severity: Caution / source review; not recommended as food
records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only until stronger species-level edible-use support is attached.
records.field_identification: Porcini-like bolete with whitish to yellow pores, firm white flesh, brown cap, and possible faint/limited blue staining. Compare against bitter Tylopilus and red/yellow boletes.
records.edibility_notes: A porcini-type edible by some accounts when confidently identified and in good condition, but this record is not source-cleared yet.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern porcini-group bolete with wrinkled grayish-brown cap, hardwood association, white flesh, and a magenta/purple ammonia reaction on the cap.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September.
pore_color: White pores becoming yellow/olive; not bruising

records.edibility_detail: Edible with expert-level porcini-group confirmation; do not use as a generic brown bolete.
records.mushroom_card_note: Eastern porcini-group bolete with wrinkled grayish-brown cap, hardwood association, white flesh, and a magenta/purple ammonia reaction on the cap.
records.field_identification: Porcini-type build, wrinkled or pocked grayish-brown cap, white pores becoming yellow/olive, prominent stem reticulation, white non-staining flesh, and hardwood association. Chemical reaction can help separate it from nearby edulis-like species.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus atkinsonii
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, oak/beech morphology, confusion with Boletus variipes-fagicola, chemical test notes, and edible-use as Choice.

edible_use.notes: Cook only confidently identified young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, waterlogged, red-pored, or strongly blue-staining lookalikes.

records.eating_use_notes: Cook only confirmed young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, waterlogged, red-pored, or strongly blue-staining lookalikes.
records.quality_notes: Potentially useful porcini-group bolete when oak/beech habitat, field traits, and lookalike checks all fit; keep expert-ID framing.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusing porcini-group boletes with bitter, red-pored, strongly blue-staining, or otherwise unsafe boletes.
records.hard_rule: Do not use as a generic brown bolete; confirm the full porcini-group field-trait set before considering food use.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

records.field_identification: Dry tan to grayish-brown cap, white young pore surface becoming yellowish or olive, non-bruising flesh and pores, and a usually prominent whitish reticulation over a brownish stem under eastern hardwoods.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd oak-associated bolete with soft brown cap, dull yellow boletinoid pores running slightly down the stem, and brown-to-reddish bruising.

research_notes: Soft brown cap, dull yellow pore surface that begins to run down the stem, slow brown to reddish-brown bruising, and brown reticulation at the stem apex. Usually associated with oaks.

records.notes: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.

mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Strong yellow reticulation over the stem, yellow pore surface, yellow flesh, and little to no blue bruising are the big field marks.

mushroom_profile.summary: Xanthoconium purpureum: purplish-red hardwood bolete close to X. affine; chemical reaction and cap color matter.
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September; an early-summer through fall oak/hickory bolete in the Midwest and eastern North America.
research_notes: Purplish to reddish-brown cap, cream-to-yellow-brown pores, no olive pore shades, and green flash with ammonia help separate it from X. affine.

records.mushroom_card_note: Small eastern hardwood Xerocomus with reddish-brown cap, yellow pores, and a reticulate upper stem; compare closely with X. tenax.
